concepts: single-threaded JS execution with a background thread pool
phases: timers, poll, check, close callbacks
questionPatterns: How does Node.js handle thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread?
What role does libuv play in Node's architecture?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains non-blocking I/O offloaded to libuv's thread pool
understands JS execution itself stays single-threaded
doesn't confuse this with multi-threading

concepts: why blocking the event loop is dangerous
questionPatterns: Why is a synchronous, CPU-heavy loop dangerous in a Node.js API?
How would you handle a CPU-intensive task without blocking the server?
evaluationCriteria: explains blocking the event loop stalls ALL requests, not just one
proposes offloading (worker threads, separate service, queue) for CPU-bound work
distinguishes I/O-bound (fine as async) from CPU-bound (needs offloading)

questionPatterns: Walk me through how you built authentication middleware in your project.
Why does middleware order matter in Express?
evaluationCriteria: explains next() and the request/response pipeline correctly
references real middleware they've written (auth, validation)
understands error-handling middleware needs 4 arguments

questionPatterns: How do you structure error handling across a large Express API?
What's the difference between an operational error and a programming bug, and should they be handled the same way?
evaluationCriteria: describes centralizing error handling rather than scattering try/catch everywhere
uses appropriate HTTP status codes in examples
distinguishes expected failures (bad input) from bugs (should crash/alert, not silently continue)

concepts: storing tokens: httpOnly cookie vs localStorage tradeoffs
questionPatterns: Walk me through your JWT authentication flow, including what happens when the access token expires.
Why would you store a JWT in an httpOnly cookie instead of localStorage?
evaluationCriteria: explains access/refresh token pattern or at least expiration handling
correctly identifies XSS risk of localStorage vs httpOnly cookie protection
understands the token is signed, not encrypted, unless explicitly using JWE
followUpConcepts: Cookies vs Local Storage vs Sessions

topic: Cookies vs Local Storage vs Sessions
concepts: session storage server-side vs client-side tokens
CSRF risk with cookies vs XSS risk with localStorage
questionPatterns: What are the security tradeoffs between storing auth tokens in cookies vs localStorage?
How would you protect against CSRF if you're using cookie-based auth?
evaluationCriteria: correctly maps XSS risk to localStorage, CSRF risk to cookies
mentions SameSite cookie attribute or CSRF tokens as mitigation
understands cookies are sent automatically which is both convenient and risky

questionPatterns: Why did your frontend get a CORS error when calling your API from a different port, and how did you fix it?
What triggers a preflight request?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains same-origin policy as the root cause
knows preflight is triggered by non-simple requests (custom headers, non-GET/POST, credentials)
references a real fix they applied (cors middleware config)
followUpConcepts: Cookies vs Local Storage vs Sessions

concepts: why rate limiting protects against abuse and DoS
questionPatterns: How did you implement rate limiting in your project, and what algorithm does it use?
Why would you rate-limit per-user instead of per-IP for an authenticated API?
evaluationCriteria: references their actual rate-limiting implementation (from Fortune Constructors project)
explains the reasoning between per-IP and per-user limiting
knows 429 is the correct status code

concepts: keeping secrets out of source control
validating required env vars at startup
questionPatterns: How do you manage different configuration values across development and production?
What happens in your app if a required environment variable is missing?
evaluationCriteria: understands secrets should never be committed to git
mentions per-environment config strategy
bonus: validates env vars exist at startup rather than failing deep in request handling

concepts: buffering data without loading it all into memory
questionPatterns: Why would you use a stream instead of reading an entire file into memory?
How does piping work between a readable and writable stream?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains memory efficiency of streaming vs full buffering
understands backpressure conceptually
gives a relevant use case (file upload, video streaming)

concepts: cluster module spawns multiple processes to use all CPU cores
worker_threads for CPU-bound parallelism within one process
questionPatterns: Node.js is single-threaded — how would you take advantage of a multi-core server?
What's the difference between the cluster module and worker_threads?
evaluationCriteria: explains cluster module forking multiple processes to use multiple cores
distinguishes worker_threads (shared memory, CPU-bound tasks) from cluster (separate processes)
gives a plausible use case for each

concepts: Socket.io adds fallback and reconnection on top of WebSockets
questionPatterns: Why did you choose Socket.io for real-time notifications instead of polling?
What does Socket.io provide on top of raw WebSockets?
evaluationCriteria: references their actual real-time notification implementation
explains persistent connection benefit over repeated polling
knows Socket.io adds reconnection/fallback handling, not just a wrapper

questionPatterns: Walk me through your file upload pipeline using Multer and Cloudinary.
How do you validate file size and type before accepting an upload?
evaluationCriteria: references their actual Multer + Cloudinary pipeline
mentions validation happening before/during upload, not after
understands multipart/form-data parsing basics

concepts: error isolation so one failure doesn't stop the whole job
questionPatterns: Walk me through your daily cron job — what does it do and how do you handle failures for individual users?
Why is idempotency important for a scheduled job?
evaluationCriteria: references their actual cron implementation (per-user error isolation)
explains why a job should be safe to re-run without duplicating data
understands failure in one iteration shouldn't crash the whole batch

concepts: why versioning matters for breaking changes
questionPatterns: How would you introduce a breaking change to an API that's already being used by a mobile app?
What are the tradeoffs between URL-based and header-based API versioning?
evaluationCriteria: proposes a valid versioning strategy
understands the core problem: can't break existing clients
can weigh at least one tradeoff between versioning approaches
